---
title: "AI Tools for Data Science and Statistics"
subtitle: "Session 3: PHI, Privacy, and Simulated-Data Workflows"
author: "Erik Westlund"
date: "June 17, 2026"
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    embed-resources: true
    incremental: true
    css: slides.css
---


# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Problem Set 2 & Practice
:::

## Problem Set 2 Practice

- For time reasons, we did not practice the modeling workflow
- This is the foundation for problem set 2
- Recommendation: do a quick effort using `practice/02_modeling-workflow`, then the real assignment

## Setting Up Problem Sets/Final Project

- You can use the practice assignments as a guide
- Set up a separate folder outside the course files
- You bring the data (you can copy it out of `data/`) and choose how to organize files
- Email me (ewestlund@jhu.edu) for support

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Context Files Summary
:::

## Context Files

- They are plain text instructions.
- Agents strategically append them to requests so the model sees project guidance.
- `AGENTS.md` (OpenAI/Other models) and `CLAUDE.md` work well for project-wide or subdirectory-wide settings.
- Skills are better for targeted actions or repeated workflows.

## Context Files: How To Use Them

- Hand-crafted context files performed better than AI-generated ones in Gloaguen et al.'s `AGENTS.md` study [@gloaguen2026agentsmd].
- Place must-have constraints in the file before work starts (but know they may not always constrain the models)
- Do not trust the agent to follow every constraint; still review the output.
- When the same papercut happens repeatedly, append a short rule to the context file.

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Session 3
:::

## Goals For Session 3

- Explain why PHI changes AI-assisted workflow design
- Separate code movement from data movement
- Use simulated data for development and review
- Identify what information can safely leave a restricted environment only after project-specific approval
- Prepare final-project workflows without exposing restricted data to AI tools

## Core Principle

Code can move across computing environments; sensitive data cannot.

## What Counts As Sensitive Here?

- Direct identifiers
- Dates, geography, rare conditions, and small cells that can become identifying
- Free text
- Linkable combinations of variables
- Restricted-use datasets even when they do not look individually identifying
- Project-specific metadata covered by agreements or policy

## Agent Risk Surface

- A chat tool sees what you paste or upload.
- An agent can inspect files it has permission to read.
- If sensitive files are in the workspace, assume the agent has access to them.
- Policy and data-use agreements determine what is allowed.

## Safe Workflow Shape

1. Work with restricted data only in the approved environment.
2. Extract only approved structural information.
3. Develop code outside the restricted environment using simulated data.
4. Review generated code before it returns to the restricted environment.
5. Run reviewed code on real data only where real data are allowed.
6. Export only approved aggregate outputs.

## Make It Impossible for AI to See Sensitive Data

- It is best that it is physically impossible for the agent to access sensitive information.

- Use a secure enclave for real data access (e.g., SAFE Desktop at Hopkins).
- Set up a virtual machine witht the sensitive data on it, where no agents could ever access it, regardless of permissions.

## Use Git To Your Advantage

- Git can help you be secure and do code transfer
- Keep your code in a git repository
- Use `.gitignore` to exclude the data directory; place your sim data here.
- `git commit` and `git push` from the AI-agent location
- `git pull` from the secure location

## Two-Folder Exercise

- `real-data/`: real data and metadata extraction; no AI.
- `sim-data/`: copied metadata, simulated data generation, and AI-assisted notebooks.

The class data are simulated, but the workflow treats `real-data/` as restricted.

## Real Data Side

- Start where the real data are allowed.
- Run `extract-data-info.R` without AI assistance.
- Capture exact column names and R data types.
- For continuous variables, capture summary statistics.
- For categorical variables, capture approved levels and distributions.
- Review `outputs/` before copying anything to `sim-data/metadata/`.

## Structural Information

Structural information may include, when allowed:

- Variable names
- Variable types
- Value labels
- Allowed categories
- Plausible ranges
- Missing-value codes
- Broad missingness patterns
- Non-disclosive row and column counts

## Sim Data Side

- Use only approved metadata copied into `metadata/`.
- Ask AI to complete `generate-sim-data.R`.
- Write simulated data to the same file name as the real data.
- Use the same column names and compatible types.
- Develop notebooks against the simulated stand-in.
- Treat simulated results as code checks, not findings.

## Information To Treat Carefully

- Exact rare category counts
- Extreme values
- Dates and timestamps
- Geographic detail
- Free-text examples
- Unusual variable combinations
- Any output that could identify a person, clinic, provider, school, or small group

## Simulated Data Still Need Review

- De-identification does not guarantee privacy.
- Do not preserve links between original IDs and simulated IDs.
- Break links between people, providers, clinics, households, schools, and dates.
- Avoid free text, rare categories, and unusually specific geography.
- Check whether simulated records recreate recognizable combinations.

## Simulated Data Purpose

Simulated data are useful for:

- Building cleaning code
- Testing joins and reshaping
- Prototyping tables and plots
- Debugging model syntax
- Reviewing workflow structure

Simulated data are not final evidence.

## No External Public Repository Requirement

This workflow does not require an external public repository.

Code can move through institution-approved mechanisms such as:

- approved internal repositories
- secure file transfer
- controlled shared drives
- manual code transfer reviewed before use

The key rule is that sensitive data do not leave the approved environment.

## In A Real Project

- You can use a single repository.
- Git-ignore data on both the real-data and sim-data sides.
- Copy only approved metadata into the AI-visible environment.
- Generate simulated data as a complete stand-in.
- Keep file names and column names identical so code moves cleanly.

## Return To Real Data

- Copy only reviewed code and notebooks back to `real-data/`.
- Do not copy simulated data back as evidence.
- Run notebooks against the real file with the same file name.
- Write final interpretation only after running on real data.
- Export only approved aggregate results.

## Hands-On Workflow

1. Run `source("updater.R")`.
2. Run `extract-data-info.R` in `real-data/` without AI.
3. Copy approved `real-data/outputs/` files to `sim-data/metadata/`.
4. Open `practice/work/03_simulated-data-privacy-workflow/sim-data/` with your AI tool.
5. Use prompts from `practice/prompts/03_simulated-data-privacy-workflow/`.
6. After review, copy notebooks back to `real-data/` and run them there without AI.

## Final Project Connection

- Develop on simulated or public data.
- Keep real or restricted data out of AI-visible workspaces.
- Submit a workflow that shows what was developed with AI assistance.
- Include a reflection on what was checked manually.
- Do not include restricted data in submitted materials.

## You Are Responsible

- AI use does not transfer responsibility to the tool.
- You are responsible for privacy, correctness, interpretation, and what you submit.
- If AI wrote code, you still need to understand what the code does.
- If you cannot explain or defend a result, do not treat it as finished.

## Use A Second Pass

- Ask a different model or a fresh chat or agent instance to review code.
- Give it the code and the intended analysis goal.
- Ask it to look for logic errors, privacy risks, statistical issues, and missing checks.
- Do not ask only "is this good?" Ask for specific failure modes.

## Cross-Check Prompt {.smaller}

```text
Review this analysis code as a skeptical statistical collaborator.

Look for:
- privacy or data exposure risks
- logic errors
- incorrect assumptions about variables
- statistical modeling issues
- missing diagnostics or sensitivity checks
- places where the code runs but answers the wrong question

Do not rewrite the code yet. First give me a prioritized list of concerns with line references.
```

## Second-Pass Caveat

- A second model can catch errors, but it can also invent concerns.
- Treat model review as triage, not validation.
- Final checks still belong to the analyst: read the code, run it, inspect outputs, and verify against the scientific question.

## Wrap-Up

- Privacy is a workflow constraint, not an afterthought.
- Agents increase the importance of file boundaries and permissions.
- Simulated data support development, not final inference.
- The analyst remains responsible for correctness, privacy, and interpretation.
